feedback.text: The catastrophic societal consequences of algorithmic addiction cannot be ignored even if the platforms meant well. These systems are designed purely to grab our attention and they are having a deeply damaging effect on society. They push users into self confirming echo chambers and speed up radicalization. They are dividing nations and feeding populism all over the world. We are more connected than ever but the app design maximizes addictive behavior and feeds an epidemic of loneliness and poor mental health. Existing laws don't go far enough. The Digital Fairness Act is an opportunity to address this structural addiction as a market failure that needs immediate, powerful action to protect our individual freedom and democratic integrity. To truly fix this the EU has the opportunity to facilitate big structural changes instead of relying on small fines. One key step is that the core algorithms which are the true engines of this societal damage could be made public and transparent. This would allow independent experts to study their real effects. The EU could also look toward creating specific and standardized metrics that measure the addiction potential and how much division or mental health harm apps are creating. Platforms could then be expected to meet a mandatory Fairness Standard. This Standard might involve legally restricting them from exceeding a defined level of addictiveness or divisive algorithmic output. We can work toward forcing platforms to design systems that put human well being before the companys profit goals
